title: Chapter 36: The Everyday
content: I burn the toast on Tuesday.

Not a gentle char — an actual, smoke-curling, fire-alarm-beeping catastrophe. The kitchen fills with the sharp, acrid smell of carbonized sourdough, and I stand there in my socks and one of Julian's hoodies, staring at the smoking toaster like it personally betrayed me.
"Again?" Julian calls from the bedroom, and there's no irritation in his voice, just the warm amusement of a man who has watched me burn toast four times this month and loves me anyway.
"The setting is wrong!" I shout back, which is a lie. The setting is the same setting it's been since we bought the thing. I am the variable.

He appears in the doorway, hair still pillow-creased on one side, guitar pick between his teeth because of course — he leaves them everywhere, scattered through the flat like small confessions of a life spent reaching for music. I've found them in the bathroom, on the kitchen counter, once inside the refrigerator which I still can't explain. He takes the pick from his mouth and grins at me.
"You want me to do it?"
"I want you to fix the toaster."
"The toaster's fine, love."
"The toaster is a liar and I hate it."

He laughs — that full-body, unguarded laugh that still makes something in my chest go loose and warm — and steps past me to rescue the bread situation. I lean against the counter and watch him. The cats, Gatsby and his newer accomplice Brioche, wind between our ankles, purring with the particular smugness of creatures who have never once been expected to make their own breakfast.
This is it. This is the life.

The mornings where I burn things and he fixes them. The afternoons where the café runs itself on autopilot and we steal moments between orders. The weekends where it requires both of us — him at the espresso machine, me at the register, our hands brushing as we pass each other mugs and change and the silent shorthand of twelve years of not-quite-knowing and one year of finally, finally getting it right.

It's ordinary. It's the exact life I was terrified of choosing because it seemed too small — too quiet, too simple, too far from the buzzing, multi-threaded existence I'd built in New York where every minute was optimized and every path was computable.
It's not small. It's the right size.
Three in the morning.

I wake the way I always do now — not with a start, not gasping, just a gentle surface. The bedroom is dark and quiet. Julian is a warm shape beside me, breathing slow and even. Brioche is a warmer shape on my feet, ten pounds of committed stillness.
My hand is already reaching for the nightstand.

Muscle memory. Months of shift-sleep-worry have carved a groove into my nervous system, and sometimes my body still follows it — reaching for the phone, the Fork app, the paths, the percentages, the constantly refreshing feed of maybes that used to be the architecture of my entire existence. My fingers find the phone, and I pause.
The screen lights up when I lift it.

No Fork app. No shifting probabilities. No branching timelines rendered in clean, terrifying UI. Just my lock screen — Julian and Gatsby in the café window, afternoon light catching the dust motes around them, both of them looking slightly confused by the camera, as if they've been interrupted in the middle of a conversation they were enjoying.
I took that photo eight months ago. I've never changed it.

I smile. Not the performative kind — the real kind, the kind that starts somewhere behind my sternum and works its way outward until it reaches my mouth. I put the phone down. The screen goes dark. I settle back into the pillow, into the warmth of him, into the quiet house where the only sound is the wind off the Atlantic and the faint, contented snore of a cat who has never once worried about alternate timelines.
The ease of this still surprises me.

Not because I expected misery — I didn't, not after everything we fought through to get here. But because I spent so long in a state of low-grade emergency that calm feels like a country I'm still learning the customs of. I keep waiting for my visa to expire. I keep waiting for someone to tap my shoulder and tell me I'm in the wrong life.
No one does.
I close my eyes. I sleep.
Saturday. Workshop day.

The community center in Clonakilty has become my second home — a slightly damp, fluorescent-lit room with not enough power outlets and more heart than any building has a right to contain. My digital skills class started with six people and a folding table. Now there are fifteen of them, ages fourteen to sixty-eight, squeezed around cluster tables with their laptops and their questions and their fierce, particular determination.

Siobhán, who is sixty-eight, can now format a spreadsheet faster than I can. Donal, who is fourteen, built a website for his uncle's fishing charter and now earns more per month than I did in my first internship. And the girl — I almost say her name but I won't, because some stories aren't mine to tell in the telling, only in the witnessing — the sixteen-year-old from the village outside Rosscarbery, the one who showed up on the first day with a secondhand laptop held together with electrical tape and defiance, the one who taught herself Python on a phone screen because the school library only had two working computers and she wasn't about to wait.
She got into Trinity. Computer science. First in her family to go to university.
She tells me after class, almost casually, like she's mentioning the weather. "I got the letter this morning."

And I — I who did not cry at my own wedding, I who held it together through three different realities and an app that showed me versions of my own heartbreak before I'd even lived it — I start crying.

Right there in the community center with the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead and the smell of instant coffee and the sound of chairs scraping as the others pack up. I cry, and I pretend I'm not crying, which is a losing battle because I'm also smiling so wide it hurts, and she's smiling too, and everyone else is pretending not to notice which is the most Irish form of kindness I've ever encountered — not looking, not commenting, just quietly making space for the feeling in the room.
This. This is what my Ivy League education was always supposed to be for.

Not optimizing hedge fund algorithms. Not building an app that could render the probability of love into a percentage. Not any of the shiny, impressive, ultimately hollow things I thought success looked like when I was twenty-two and thought the future was something you computed rather than something you made.
Giving a kid from West Cork a shot at a future she built herself, and just — being in the room when she realized she'd arrived.
I hug her. She lets me. Then she pulls back and says, very seriously, "You've got mascara on your sleeve," and I laugh so hard I almost cry again.
Friday night. The garden.

Julian plays at the café now — not hiding, not performing in that desperate way he used to when the music was still a wound he was poking at. Just sharing. The way you share something that's part of you: reflexively, generously, with no expectation of what comes back.

People come from neighboring towns. Skibbereen, Bandon, Kinsale. They fill the garden with camping chairs and blankets and the particular attentive hush of people who came to listen. The July sky goes purple behind him — that impossible Irish purple, bruised and violet and edged with gold where the sun is failing — and the fairy lights we strung along the garden wall glow warmer as the light fades, and Julian sits on his stool with his guitar and he sings.

He sings the old ones — the covers everyone knows, the ones people hum along to. And he sings his own, the new ones, the ones that came back when he stopped being afraid of silence. They're not about loss anymore. Not only about loss. They're about what comes after. They're about staying.

I sit in my usual spot — the bench by the herb garden, where the rosemary and lavender release their scent when the evening dew hits them. Sweet and green and faintly medicinal. The air carries the last warmth of the day and the first cool of the night, and his voice wraps around all of it.
I had three chances at love.

Three branches. Three realities. Three different men who might have been right in different configurations of the universe. And I chose this one. I chose the one who sings in a garden in West Cork while the sky turns purple and the rosemary blooms and our cats are probably destroying something inside the café at this exact moment.
That's not a compromise.
That's a revelation.
We argue on Thursday.

About money, because the café is stable but not wealthy and there's a crack in the espresso machine that's going to need professional repair, and professional repair means professional prices, and professional prices mean looking at the budget again with tightened lips and a calculator.

About family, because Margaret wants us for Christmas and my parents want us for Christmas and we can't be in two countries at once unless the Fork app decides to make a comeback, which it hasn't, and the conversation circles like it always does — gently, then less gently, then with a sharpness that surprises us both.

About the espresso machine, because it's always his turn to clean it and he always forgets and I always do it and he always apologizes with scones, which — okay, the scones are incredible, but the point is the principle, Julian, the principle of shared responsibility in a household that we built together, and he nods and agrees and I can see him already forgetting, and I want to be angry about it but I also kind of love that about him, which makes me angrier.
"You're smiling," he says, suspicious.
"I'm not."
"You are. You're smiling right now in the middle of being angry with me."
"I'm not smiling. This is my face."
"Your face is a terrible liar."

I throw a dish towel at him. He catches it — of course he catches it, his reflexes are absurd — and uses it to pull me closer, and I let him, which is probably losing the argument but I've never once claimed to be consistent.
The arguments aren't signs that something's wrong.

I need to say that again, because I spent a long time believing that friction meant failure — that if a relationship required work, it was already broken. That's the lie the movies tell you. That's the lie I told myself when I was running probabilities and optimizing outcomes: that the right choice would be frictionless. Easy. A path with no resistance.

But friction is proof of contact. Two real people, in a real life, building something solid enough to disagree about. The arguments mean we're both still here. We're both still invested enough to fight.
He apologizes with scones the next morning. Blueberry. Still warm.
I forgive him. Obviously.
A perfectly ordinary evening.

The dinner dishes are done — Dalhousie noodles, my grandmother's recipe, the one I've finally learned to make without calling my mother for the ratios. The cats are fed and drowsing in overlapping circles on the sofa. The café is closed and quiet below us, the faint trace of espresso steam still lingering in the stairwell.

Julian is in the window seat, a book open in his lap, the lamp catching the grey at his temples and the quiet focus in his face. He's not reading — he's at that point where reading has become background for thinking, his eyes steady on the page but his mind somewhere else, somewhere musical probably, working out a progression or a lyric in that interior space I've learned to recognize and not interrupt.

I'm at the counter with my laptop, answering workshop emails. Scheduling, curriculum planning, the gentle administrative work of something that matters. My fingers move across the keyboard. My mind is half on the email and half on nothing at all — that pleasant idling mode the brain finds when the body is content.
I look up.

I don't know why. Some micro-instinct, some atavistic pull. But I look up and I look at him — really look, the way you can only look at someone after you've chosen them every day for a year. Not the scan of assessment, not the gaze of desire, not the searching look of doubt. Just the look of knowing. The look that says: I see you. I still see you. I choose to keep seeing you.
He looks up at the same moment.
Our eyes meet.

Nothing dramatic happens. No orchestral swell. No cinematic lighting shift. No glitch in the fabric of reality, no percentage ticking upward on a phone screen. Just two people in a small flat above a café in West Cork, looking at each other across twelve feet of ordinary space.
Everything dramatic happens.
He smiles. That smile — unhurried and certain, the one that reaches his eyes and softens everything around it, the one that says I'm here and I'm staying and I am so glad you're here too.
I smile back.

And I think: I used to live in three worlds. I used to split myself across branches and probabilities and the paralyzing infinite calculus of what-ifs. I used to believe that choosing meant losing — that every path taken was a path mourned, that commitment was a kind of death.

Now I live in one world. This one. The one where I burn toast and he leaves guitar picks in the bathroom and the cats are menaces and the café needs us on weekends and the espresso machine is a recurring diplomatic crisis and the sky goes purple over a garden where my husband sings on Friday nights.
It's the only one I need.
I look back down at my laptop. He looks back down at his book. The cats purr. The clock ticks. The Atlantic wind moves through the dark outside.

And underneath the ordinary — supporting it, sustaining it, making it possible — there is love. Not the kind that arrives with fanfare and glitching realities. The kind that stays. The kind you wake up to. The kind that burns toast and makes scones and teaches kids to code and argues about espresso machines and meets your eyes across a room on a night when nothing happens and everything does.
The kind you choose.
Every single day.
